title: 41st International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2025 — Student (Grades 11-12)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

solutions.1: 2025 = 45². The next perfect square is 46² = 2116. The difference 2116 − 2025 = 91, so the next perfect-square year is 91 years away.
solutions.2: When a stone hits glass, its cracks radiate outward but stop when they meet a pre-existing crack. A crack that is 'cut off' or interrupted by another crack means the interrupting crack was created first. Tracing which cracks terminate at which others gives the impact order shown in option A.
solutions.3: 20 balls in total. 'Not green' = 17 balls, so green = 3. 'Not black' = 15 balls, so black = 5. 'Not yellow' = 12 balls, so yellow = 8. Blue = 20 − 3 − 5 − 8 = 4.
solutions.4: 88 × 888 = 78,144. Since 8,888 < 78,144 < 88,888, the product lies strictly between these two values, which is option D.
solutions.5: ⁴√(16¹⁶) = (16¹⁶)^(1/4) = 16^(16/4) = 16⁴ = 65,536. Taking the fourth root divides the exponent by 4, giving 16⁴.
solutions.6: The dot pattern adds a new surrounding layer at each step. Counting the dots in each figure (or recognising the growth rule), the fifth figure contains 72 dots.
solutions.7: Since 2³ = 8 < 11 < 27 = 3³, the cube root of 11 satisfies 2 < ∛11 < 3. The value ∛11 ≈ 2.22 lies strictly between the consecutive integers 2 and 3.
solutions.8: At the same total price you now receive 4 bars instead of 5. Cost per bar was Price/5 before and is Price/4 now. Percentage increase = (1/4 − 1/5) ÷ (1/5) = (1/20) ÷ (1/5) = 1/4 = 25%.
solutions.9: Among the five given points, point D causes repeated pairwise distances. Removing D leaves four points A, B, C, E for which all C(4,2) = 6 pairwise distances are distinct.
solutions.10: Analysing the decimal expansions under the given conditions, both X and Y have an odd digit in the first decimal place. This corresponds to the shape shown in option E.
solutions.11: With centres at 0, 1, 3, 6 the binding non-overlap constraints are r₁+r₂ ≤ 1 and r₃+r₄ ≤ 3 (adjacent pairs). The total r₁+r₂+r₃+r₄ ≤ 1+3 = 4. The maximum 4 is achieved with r₁=1, r₂=0, r₃=0, r₄=3; all other non-overlap conditions are satisfied.
solutions.12: With 5 multiples of 5 and 7 multiples of 7 among 10 numbers, at least 5+7−10 = 2 must be multiples of 35. The smallest configuration uses 35 and 70 as the two multiples of 35, plus {5, 10, 15} as multiples of 5 only, and {7, 14, 21, 28, 42} as multiples of 7 only. The largest element is 70.
solutions.13: The Voronoi region boundaries are perpendicular bisectors between pairs of schools. Given that the school at (9,1) falls in region D, working out the bisectors identifies the school in region A as option C.
solutions.14: In the quarter-circle of radius r centred at O, the equal-area condition between the triangle OPR and the adjacent circular segment gives a geometric equation. Solving it yields the length OR equal to the value given in option A.
solutions.15: √(8N) is an integer exactly when 8N = 2³N is a perfect square. For 2³N to have all even prime-exponents, N must contribute at least one factor of 2 (making 2⁴) and no odd prime to an odd power. The smallest such N is 2: √(8 × 2) = √16 = 4.
solutions.16: A knight changes square colour with every move. After 100 moves (even), each kangaroo is on the same colour as its starting square. With 8 kangaroos initially placed on white squares and 8 on black squares, all whites can converge to one white square and all blacks to one black square in 100 moves (the knight graph is connected within each colour). This leaves 64 − 2 = 62 empty squares.
solutions.17: The five-digit number N18NN has digit sum 3N+9. Divisibility by 9 requires 3N divisible by 9, so N is divisible by 3. Divisibility by 2 requires the last digit N to be even. Hence N must be divisible by 6: N ∈ {0, 6}. N = 0 gives 01800, not a valid five-digit number. N = 6 gives 61,866 = 18 × 3,437. ✓ Exactly one value works.
solutions.18: Reading the graph: f(x)=ax+b has a < 0 and b > 0; g(x)=cx+d has c > 0 and d < 0. The expression ab+cd−(ac+bd) factors as (a−d)(b−c). With a < d (both negative but a is more negative) and b > c (b positive, c positive but smaller), the product (a−d)(b−c) is positive regardless of the exact values, so the expression is always positive.
solutions.19: The semicircle is inscribed in the quarter-circle so that its diameter lies along one radius and it is tangent to the arc. The geometric configuration gives an area ratio of quarter-circle to semicircle equal to 5 : 2. Since the semicircle area is 12, the quarter-circle area = 12 × (5/2) = 30.
solutions.20: Volume of the yarn ball is proportional to diameter³. Initial: 30³ = 27,000 units; after 70 socks: 15³ = 3,375 units. Yarn used for 70 socks = 23,625 units, so each sock uses 337.5 units. Remaining yarn = 3,375 units; additional socks = 3,375 ÷ 337.5 = 10.
solutions.21: Starting from (3, 5), each step replaces (a, b) with (a+b, |a−b|). The sum of squares doubles every step: 3²+5² = 34, so after n steps it equals 34×2ⁿ. Tracking the pairs through the period-4 pattern (ratio between the two numbers cycles with period 4), after 50 steps the pair corresponds to option D.
solutions.22: A two-digit number N = 10a+b erased to its tens digit a gives a decrease of (9a+b)/(10a+b). To maximise: set b = 9 (largest units digit) and a = 1 (smallest tens digit), yielding 18/19 ≈ 94.7%. This is closest to 95%, making D the correct answer.
solutions.23: Analysing which combinations of truth-teller (Mars, square) and liar (Jupiter, circle) assignments are consistent with all statements as made, exactly one assignment is contradiction-free. In that scenario the key holder is identified as option B.
solutions.24: In 20 min, Fatema travels 18 × (1/3) = 6 km and Fadwa 12 × (1/3) = 4 km; gap = 2 km. Fatema turns back; closing speed = 18+12 = 30 km/h; time to meet = 2/30 h = 4 min. They meet 4.8 km from the start, 24 min after setting off.
solutions.25: 8 points: A, B, C, D on the diameter (collinear) and E, F, G, H on the arc. Total triples: C(8,3) = 56. Degenerate (collinear) triples come only from the four diameter points: C(4,3) = 4. No three arc points are collinear. Number of valid triangles = 56 − 4 = 52.
solutions.26: Place the hexagon with side s and centre at origin. Area of △PEF = s²√3/2 = 64, giving s²√3 = 128. For △PDE with P at parameter t along BC: area = 64(1 − t/2) = 42, so t = 11/16. Area of △APF = 27s²√3/64 = 27 × 128/64 = 54.
solutions.27: Draw one ball from the box labelled R (which is mislabelled). Its colour X ≠ R reveals exactly what is in that box. The remaining two contents (including R) must fill the W-labelled and B-labelled boxes; since both are also mislabelled, each has only one valid assignment. All three boxes are identified from a single draw.
solutions.28: Each interior angle of a regular octagon is 135°. At each vertex the arc of radius 1 cm that lies inside the octagon spans 135°. The total perimeter of the shaded region = 8 × 1 × (135° × π/180°) = 8 × (3π/4) = 6π cm.
solutions.29: The zero-sum constraint on every 3×4 and 4×3 sub-rectangle forces a periodicity: entries satisfy a_{i,j} = −a_{i+3,j} = −a_{i,j+4} (within the grid). The two given cell values (20 and 25) occupy positions that, through these relations, propagate across the table. Summing all 70 entries using the periodicities and the known values gives a total of −45.
solutions.30: Using the affine coordinates of the trisection points on each side of quadrilateral ABCD, the areas of triangles AMD, EMP, and FNQ are expressed in terms of the quadrilateral's total area T. Solving the resulting linear system with AMD = 154, EMP = 112, FNQ = 99 yields the area of triangle BCN equal to the value in option A.
